segments.text: Thank you, Madam Chair. Morning. Thank you for giving me an opportunity to talk.
I see telecommunications as an everyday utility. It has become a social requirement
for us in our global age. I believe it is essential to life.
The governments withdrawal from regulation, indeed the provision, has had social consequences.
I believe that some things are best managed by private business or by the commercialised,
commercialised, but not everything, and especially not this. Brian Easton said in his book,
The Whimpering of the State, a treatment of the telecommunications under neoliberalism,
describes that the process as flawed detail overburdened by Chicago school ideology,
the result has been an adequate framework for dealing with anti-competitive
activities and natural monopolies. Bad ideas have bad consequences. Where policies touch
to lives of people, they can leave long scars or deep streams of enrichment. History will judge them
for the success they have had for creating wellbeing and flourishing in their societies
or misery and hardship. I would like to bring up the dynamic pricing model. Under the revenue
management, it provides a lucrative way for providers to increase profitability from their
products and services. It is not possible for people to understand of you how a service or product
price has been calculated. There is a huge difference between the cost of producing the product
versus the margin which the red-tailor has added.
I'm not anti-business.
The telecommunications market has created big profits
for the private investor at the expense of customers
and this is not success.
I argue that in the public interest,
this is not sustainable or desirable
to manage something that is tantamount
to an essential social requirement.
I want us to see us to use a pricing
which is not formulated inside the market model
or customer demand and supply
but instead is formulated inside the cost
of producing a service or product,
what is commonly known as cost plus pricing.
What this would do is provide us with transparency for users.
I see any attempt to reform the industry
short of doing this as a failure
whether this means a program of nationalisation
or something else.
Questions?
Thank you, Matt.
From what I'm hearing I think you're talking about
telecommunications being public good
and we need to find a way to ensure
that public good is accessible to everyone.
That's pretty much what you're saying.
So this public utility,
Matt, I think ultimately I'd like a lot of things
such as water power all managed by some state
and I guess in the case of what we had 50 years ago,
I'm turning, I'm 50 this year
and this is what I was,
this is a system that has happened over my lifetime.
So yeah.
Thank you so much, Max, for your contribution.
Thank you.
As the members we quickly move into closed session.
